statement: Bananas are berries, but strawberries are not.
explanation: Botanically, bananas develop from one flower with one ovary and have seeds inside. Strawberries are aggregate fruits, which is rude but accurate.

statement: Lightning avoids places it has already struck.
explanation: Lightning can strike the same place many times. Tall buildings and towers get repeat visits like they owe the sky money.

statement: Octopuses have three hearts.
explanation: Two hearts pump blood to the gills, and one pumps it to the rest of the body. That is a lot of emotional infrastructure.

statement: Most of the human brain stays inactive during ordinary thinking.
explanation: Brain imaging shows we use many brain regions across ordinary tasks. The 10 percent idea is a movie-friendly myth.

statement: A group of flamingos can be called a flamboyance.
explanation: It is a real collective noun, and honestly the birds earned it.

statement: A goldfish forgets a new routine within a few seconds.
explanation: Goldfish can learn routines and remember things far longer than three seconds. The insult is doing most of the swimming here.

statement: The Eiffel Tower can grow taller in hot weather.
explanation: Metal expands when heated, so the tower can rise several inches on hot days. Summer gives it a little stretch.

statement: Bats are blind.
explanation: Bats can see, and many also use echolocation. They are not flying around on vibes alone.

statement: Honey can last for thousands of years without spoiling if sealed well.
explanation: Honey's low moisture and acidity make it hostile to many microbes. Ancient honey can still be recognizable honey.

statement: The Great Wall of China is clearly visible from the Moon with the naked eye.
explanation: Astronauts have repeatedly pushed back on this myth. From the Moon, human-made structures are not easy naked-eye targets.

statement: Sharks existed before trees.
explanation: Shark ancestors are hundreds of millions of years old, older than the earliest forests. Sharks were vintage before shade was trendy.

statement: Coffee beans are actually beans.
explanation: They are seeds from coffee cherries. We call them beans because apparently breakfast needed more confusion.

statement: There is a species of jellyfish often described as biologically immortal.
explanation: Turritopsis dohrnii can revert to an earlier life stage under stress. It is less superhero and more extreme reset button.

statement: Mount Everest is the closest point on Earth to the Moon.
explanation: Because Earth bulges at the equator, Mount Chimborazo in Ecuador reaches farther from Earth's center than Everest.

statement: A day on Venus is longer than a year on Venus.
explanation: Venus rotates very slowly, taking longer to spin once than to orbit the Sun. Its calendar is a scheduling nightmare.

statement: Chameleons mainly change color to match any background perfectly.
explanation: Color changes often signal mood, temperature, light, or communication. They are not tiny living paint samples.

statement: Wombat poop is cube-shaped.
explanation: Wombats produce unusually cube-like droppings, likely shaped by their intestines. Nature has very specific hobbies.

statement: Sugar makes kids hyper.
explanation: Studies have not found a consistent sugar-hyperactivity effect. Birthday parties may be the real culprit.

statement: The shortest war in recorded history lasted under an hour.
explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 is commonly cited at about 38 to 45 minutes. Some meetings last longer and do less.

statement: Fortune cookies were invented in China.
explanation: Modern fortune cookies are strongly associated with Japanese and Chinese immigrant businesses in the United States, especially California.

statement: A shrimp's heart is located in its head area.
explanation: A shrimp's heart sits in the cephalothorax, near what people casually call the head. Shrimp anatomy is not here for our categories.

statement: Penguins live naturally at the North Pole.
explanation: Wild penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere. The Arctic has polar bears; penguins wisely skipped that neighborhood.

statement: The first oranges were not necessarily orange.
explanation: Some oranges can stay greenish in warm climates even when ripe. The fruit and the color had a branding meeting later.

statement: U.S. courts have treated tomatoes as fruit when applying import taxes.
explanation: Botanically it is a fruit, but the U.S. Supreme Court treated tomatoes as vegetables for tariff purposes in 1893.

statement: Sound travels faster in water than in air.
explanation: Sound moves faster through water because its particles transmit vibrations more efficiently than air.

statement: The tongue has separate zones for sweet, salty, sour, and bitter tastes.
explanation: Taste receptors are distributed broadly across the tongue. The old tongue map is a classroom fossil.

statement: Some turtles can breathe through their rear end.
explanation: Some aquatic turtles can absorb oxygen through cloacal bursae. It is called cloacal respiration, because science says things directly.

statement: The Sahara Desert is the largest desert on Earth.
explanation: Antarctica is the largest desert by precipitation standards. Deserts are about dryness, not just sand drama.

statement: The dot over a lowercase i or j has a name.
explanation: It is called a tittle. Tiny dot, excellent vocabulary.

statement: Cracking your knuckles causes arthritis.
explanation: Research has not shown that knuckle cracking causes arthritis. It may annoy nearby people, which is a separate diagnosis.

statement: Century years can skip being leap years.
explanation: Years like 1900 are not leap years unless they are divisible by 400. Calendar rules enjoy tiny plot twists.

statement: The North Star is the brightest star in the night sky.
explanation: Polaris is useful for navigation, but Sirius is brighter. Fame and brightness are different departments.

statement: Some cats are allergic to humans.
explanation: Cats can react to human dander or products people use. The judgmental look may have paperwork behind it.

statement: A peanut is a true tree nut.
explanation: Peanuts are legumes, related to beans and peas. They just hang out in nut mixes.

statement: The human body has more bacterial cells than human cells by a huge 10-to-1 margin.
explanation: That old ratio has been revised downward; current estimates are much closer. Still plenty of microscopic roommates.

statement: An adult human has fewer bones than a newborn baby.
explanation: Babies have around 270 bones, and many fuse as they grow, leaving adults with 206.

statement: Napoleon Bonaparte was extremely short for his time.
explanation: He was around average height for a Frenchman of his era. The short-Napoleon idea got a lot of help from propaganda.

statement: The word laser started as an acronym.
explanation: LASER comes from Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Somehow the acronym is easier.

statement: Camels store water in their humps.
explanation: Camel humps store fat, not water. The water-saving magic is mostly in their physiology.

statement: The unicorn is the national animal of Scotland.
explanation: Scotland has used the unicorn as a heraldic symbol for centuries. Completely imaginary, fully official energy.

statement: The five Olympic rings represent the five original Olympic sports.
explanation: The rings represent the union of inhabited continents and athletes meeting from around the world, not five sports.

statement: Some metals can explode when dropped into water.
explanation: Alkali metals like sodium and potassium react violently with water. Do not season soup with laboratory sodium.

statement: A desert is classified by heat rather than rainfall.
explanation: Deserts are defined by low precipitation, so cold deserts exist too. Antarctica is the chilly champion.

statement: The plastic tips on shoelaces are called aglets.
explanation: Aglets keep laces from fraying and make them easier to thread. Tiny heroes, weird name.

statement: A dog sweats mainly through its tongue.
explanation: Dogs cool themselves by panting, but their sweat glands are mostly in their paw pads.

statement: Glass is technically an amorphous solid, not a very slow liquid.
explanation: Old windows are uneven because of old manufacturing, not because glass slowly flowed down the pane.

statement: The color red makes bulls angry.
explanation: Bulls react to movement more than color. The cape is theatrical, not personally offensive.

statement: The first product with a barcode scanned in a store was a pack of gum.
explanation: A pack of Wrigley's gum was famously scanned in Ohio in 1974. Tiny gum, giant retail moment.

statement: At a mountaintop campsite, water boils hotter than it does at sea level.
explanation: That is true at sea level under standard pressure. Change altitude or pressure, and the boiling point changes.

statement: The hashtag symbol is also called an octothorpe.
explanation: Octothorpe is a real name for #. It sounds like a tiny wizard but is punctuation.

statement: Balance is handled by the five classic senses.
explanation: Beyond the classic five, we sense balance, body position, temperature, pain, and more.

statement: A blue whale's heart can weigh as much as a small car.
explanation: Blue whale hearts are enormous and can weigh hundreds of pounds. Ocean cardio is serious business.

statement: The microwave oven was invented after a candy bar melted near radar equipment.
explanation: Percy Spencer noticed microwaves could heat food after radar equipment melted candy in his pocket.

statement: You can see the color pink as one wavelength of light.
explanation: Pink is perceived from a mix of wavelengths, especially red and blue/violet. There is no single pink wavelength in the rainbow.

statement: The word robot comes from a Czech word connected to forced labor.
explanation: Robot was popularized by Karel Capek's play R.U.R. and comes from robota, meaning forced labor or drudgery.

statement: An ostrich hides its head in the sand when scared.
explanation: Ostriches do not bury their heads to hide. They may lower their heads near nests, which looks suspicious from far away.

statement: Some clouds can weigh more than a million pounds.
explanation: Cloud droplets are tiny and spread out, but together they can add up to enormous mass.

statement: A flight recorder's nickname comes from its dark exterior.
explanation: Flight recorders are usually bright orange to make them easier to find after an accident. The nickname is dramatic, not descriptive.

statement: A jiffy is sometimes used as a real unit of time.
explanation: In different technical contexts, a jiffy can mean a tiny specific time interval. Grandma was almost doing physics.

statement: Eating carrots gives humans night vision.
explanation: Carrots support eye health because of vitamin A, but they will not turn you into a snack-powered owl.

statement: Nintendo started as a company that made playing cards.
explanation: Nintendo began in Kyoto in 1889 making hanafuda playing cards. Before plumbers and princesses, there were cards.

statement: Bubble wrap was first pitched as textured wallpaper.
explanation: Its inventors originally tried to sell the air-bubble plastic as wallpaper. The wall decor failed, but the shipping world got a poppable hero.

statement: Play-Doh began as a cleaner for wallpaper.
explanation: The compound was first used to lift soot from wallpaper before it was reworked into a children's modeling toy.

statement: QR codes were invented to help track car parts.
explanation: Denso Wave created QR codes in Japan in the 1990s for automotive manufacturing. Restaurant menus were not the original villain.

statement: The first computer mouse had a wooden shell.
explanation: Douglas Engelbart's early mouse prototype used a small wooden case. Very high tech, very shop class.

statement: Bluetooth is named after a Scandinavian king.
explanation: Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a king associated with uniting parts of Denmark and Norway. The tech name is a historical nerd wink.

statement: Velcro was inspired by burrs sticking to clothing and fur.
explanation: Engineer George de Mestral studied burrs under magnification and turned their hook-like structure into a fastener idea.

statement: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire.
explanation: Teaching at Oxford goes back to the 1000s, while the Aztec Empire rose centuries later. Medieval homework beat them to it.

statement: Cleopatra lived closer in time to the first Moon landing than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
explanation: The Great Pyramid was already ancient in Cleopatra's day. She is closer to NASA than to pyramid construction on the timeline.

statement: High heels were worn by Persian horsemen before becoming European fashion.
explanation: Heeled footwear helped riders brace in stirrups. Later, European elites made it a fashion flex.

statement: The tiny pocket on jeans was originally meant for pocket watches.
explanation: That little denim pocket comes from a time when watch pockets were useful, not just mysterious.

statement: Many restaurant wasabi servings are mostly horseradish.
explanation: Real wasabi is expensive and delicate, so many places serve a horseradish-based substitute with green color.

statement: Cashews grow attached to a fruit often called a cashew apple.
explanation: The cashew nut grows below a swollen fruit-like stem called a cashew apple. It looks like the nut is hanging on for dear life.

statement: The first webcam watched a coffee pot.
explanation: Researchers at Cambridge used an early webcam to check whether the coffee pot had coffee before walking over. Noble technology.

statement: The Celsius and Fahrenheit scales meet at -40 degrees.
explanation: -40 degrees is the same temperature in Celsius and Fahrenheit. It is also the temperature where everyone agrees to stay inside.

statement: The word robot entered English through a Czech play.
explanation: Karel Capek's 1920 play R.U.R. helped introduce robot to English from a Czech word tied to forced labor.

statement: The Monopoly mascot wears a monocle.
explanation: Rich Uncle Pennybags has a top hat and mustache, but no monocle. Your brain may be borrowing one from Mr. Peanut.

statement: Darth Vader says, "Luke, I am your father."
explanation: The famous line is actually, "No, I am your father." The misquote has been doing heavy breathing for decades.

statement: Sherlock Holmes says "Elementary, my dear Watson" in the original stories.
explanation: Holmes says versions of "elementary" and "my dear Watson," but the exact famous phrase was popularized later.

statement: White chocolate gets its color from cocoa powder.
explanation: White chocolate uses cocoa butter, not cocoa solids. That is why it is pale and why chocolate arguments begin.

statement: Pineapples grow on trees.
explanation: Pineapples grow from low plants near the ground. The tree version is tropical fan fiction.

statement: Vikings wore horned helmets into battle.
explanation: The horned-helmet image mostly comes from later art and stage costumes, not battlefield evidence.

statement: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built by Romans.
explanation: The Great Pyramid was built in ancient Egypt thousands of years before Rome became an empire.

statement: The first iPhone launched with an App Store.
explanation: The original iPhone launched in 2007, and the App Store arrived in 2008. Early iPhone life was weirdly app-less.

statement: The @ symbol was invented for email addresses.
explanation: The @ symbol existed long before email. Email gave it a comeback tour, not a birth certificate.

statement: Microwave ovens were invented to dry wet newspapers faster.
explanation: The microwave oven story traces to radar equipment heating food, famously melting candy near engineer Percy Spencer.

statement: A person weighs slightly more at the equator than at the poles.
explanation: Because of Earth's rotation and shape, a person weighs slightly less at the equator than at the poles.

statement: Astronauts can chat through open space without radios if they are close enough.
explanation: Sound needs a medium like air to travel. In open space, radios do the talking.

statement: The first email was sent from a smartphone.
explanation: Email predates smartphones by decades. The first networked email experiments happened on early computer networks.

statement: The Q in QR code stands for quality.
explanation: QR stands for Quick Response. The code was designed to be scanned quickly, not to compliment itself.

statement: A piano has fewer strings than keys.
explanation: Most pianos have more strings than keys because many notes use multiple strings. The instrument is secretly a string party.

statement: The first printed book made with movable metal type in Europe was a cookbook.
explanation: Gutenberg is famous for printing the Bible with movable type in Europe, not a medieval casserole collection.

prompt: In Benjamin Franklin's famous bird complaint, which bird did he call a creature of 'bad moral character'?

choices.text: The Canada goose, somehow already acting suspicious

explanation: Franklin privately criticized the bald eagle and praised the turkey as more respectable. He did not officially nominate the turkey, which is a missed mascot opportunity.

prompt: Before people called it the Liberty Bell, what was the bell commonly known as?
explanation: It hung in the Pennsylvania State House, so the original name was practical rather than inspirational. Branding arrived later.

prompt: Where was George Washington when the Declaration of Independence was being signed in Philadelphia?

choices.text: In New York with the Continental Army
On a Philadelphia balcony waiting for his turn
At Mount Vernon testing a new plow
In Boston asking if anyone saved the tea receipt

explanation: Washington was commanding the army in New York, not signing in Philadelphia. Founding a country involved scheduling conflicts.

prompt: What did Congress actually do with the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776?

choices.text: Rang the Liberty Bell for live coverage
Mailed the king a glittery breakup card

explanation: July 4 marks approval of the text. Most of the famous signing came later, because even revolutions have paperwork lag.

prompt: When did many delegates sign the engrossed Declaration of Independence?

choices.text: July 4, 1776, during one dramatic group signing
April 1, because the king needed a prank

explanation: The formal parchment signing mostly happened on August 2. July 4 still gets the fireworks, which feels politically efficient.

prompt: Congress originally sent the states how many proposed amendments in what became the Bill of Rights package?

choices.text: Twenty-seven, one for each powdered wig

explanation: Congress proposed twelve amendments; ten were ratified in 1791 as the Bill of Rights. Two got voted off the island.

prompt: On the Great Seal of the United States, what does the eagle hold?

choices.text: A flag and a quill pen
A bell and a map of Philadelphia

explanation: The arrows represent war and the olive branch represents peace. The eagle is basically holding the national mood board.

prompt: Who designed the 50-star U.S. flag that became official?

choices.text: High school student Robert G. Heft
A patriotic bird with excellent spacing instincts

explanation: Robert G. Heft designed it as a school project. The assignment became the flag, which is a pretty aggressive extra-credit outcome.

prompt: The United States bought Alaska from which country?
explanation: The 1867 Alaska Purchase was from Russia. Critics mocked it as Seward's Folly, which aged poorly once Alaska kept being Alaska.

prompt: The Louisiana Purchase was bought from whom?

choices.text: Napoleon's cousin with a suspicious briefcase

explanation: The United States bought the territory from France in 1803. Napoleon apparently looked at a giant map and said cash sounded nice.

prompt: Which president was the first born after the United States declared independence?
explanation: Van Buren was born in 1782, making him the first president born after independence. Earlier presidents were colonial-era babies.

prompt: What structure knocked the Washington Monument out of the "world's tallest" title?

choices.text: The Statue of Liberty standing on tiptoe
The Brooklyn Bridge, by being dramatic

explanation: The Washington Monument briefly held the title after completion, then the Eiffel Tower took it in 1889. Tallness is a competitive hobby.

prompt: How many justices did the Supreme Court have when it first met?
explanation: The Court started with six justices. The familiar number nine came later, after Congress changed the size more than once.

prompt: What was the name of George Washington's Revolutionary War spy network around New York?
explanation: The Culper Spy Ring passed intelligence to Washington. The Revolution had code names, invisible ink, and absolutely no chill.

prompt: George Washington's dentures were not wooden. What were they made from?

choices.text: Ivory, human and animal teeth, and metal

explanation: The wooden-teeth story is a myth. The real materials were more complicated, more expensive, and honestly more unsettling.

prompt: Which American victory helped convince France to formally ally with the United States?

choices.text: The Boston Tea Party, judged on splash quality

explanation: Saratoga showed France the American cause had a real shot. International friendship sometimes needs a battlefield receipt.

prompt: Which winter encampment became famous for the Continental Army's brutal hardship and training?
explanation: Valley Forge was miserable, but it also became a training ground. The team-building exercise was hypothermia with paperwork.

prompt: Which battle effectively ended major fighting in the Revolutionary War?
explanation: The British surrender at Yorktown in 1781 effectively ended major fighting, though the formal peace treaty came later.

prompt: Paul Revere's famous ride was not a solo mission. Which two riders are often named with him?

choices.text: Thunder and Committee Meeting, two very tired horses

explanation: Revere was part of a larger alarm system. Dawes and Prescott deserve more than a historical "also starring."

prompt: At the Boston Tea Party, whose tea did protesters dump into the harbor?

choices.text: The British East India Company's tea
King George III's private breakfast stash

explanation: The tea belonged to the East India Company. The protest was political, caffeinated, and extremely bad for harbor flavor.

prompt: What was the first U.S. capital city under the Constitution?
explanation: New York City was the first capital under the Constitution. D.C. had to wait its turn like everyone else.

prompt: Who made "White House" the official name of the president's residence?
explanation: Theodore Roosevelt made the name official in 1901. Before that, the building had more aliases than a spy ring.

prompt: The words to "The Star-Spangled Banner" were set to which tune?
explanation: Francis Scott Key's poem was paired with an existing tune. National anthems apparently can have cover-song energy.

prompt: What color was the Statue of Liberty before weather turned it green?
explanation: The statue is copper, and its green patina formed over time. Liberty aged into her signature look.

prompt: For a while after dedication, the Statue of Liberty also served what practical job?
explanation: It served as a lighthouse, though not a very effective one. Symbolism: excellent. Beam strength: needs improvement.

prompt: Who wrote the Pledge of Allegiance?

choices.text: The Committee of Very Serious Hats

explanation: Francis Bellamy wrote the pledge in 1892. Washington was busy being long deceased.

prompt: When were the words "under God" added to the Pledge of Allegiance?

choices.text: Whenever the rhyming committee found room

explanation: Congress added the words in 1954 during the Cold War era. The pledge had a patch update.

prompt: Which document served as the United States' first national constitution before the current Constitution?

choices.text: A group text called "New Country Who Dis"

explanation: The Articles of Confederation came first and created a very weak central government. The Constitution was the redesign.

prompt: Where did the Constitutional Convention meet in 1787?

choices.text: A secret basement under a tavern

explanation: The Convention met in Philadelphia. The weather was hot, the windows were closed, and compromise smelled difficult.

prompt: The Federalist Papers were published under what shared pen name?
explanation: Hamilton, Madison, and Jay wrote as Publius. Anonymous political posting has been with us for a while.

prompt: Who wrote the largest number of Federalist Papers essays?
explanation: Hamilton wrote the most essays. The man saw a blank page and attacked.

prompt: What was the name of the first printed broadside of the Declaration of Independence?
explanation: Printer John Dunlap produced the first broadside copies. Before screenshots, you needed a printer with revolutionary deadlines.

prompt: Who wrote the pamphlet "Common Sense"?

choices.text: An anonymous chicken with a printing press

explanation: Thomas Paine wrote "Common Sense," a wildly influential argument for independence. Subtle title, not subtle impact.

prompt: Who wrote to John Adams in 1776 asking him to "remember the ladies"?

choices.text: The Ladies Remembering Committee, with excellent stationery

explanation: Abigail Adams wrote the famous line to John Adams. Founding-era group chat would have been spicy.

prompt: Who disguised herself as a man and served in the Continental Army?
explanation: Deborah Sampson served disguised as a man. The Revolution had plot twists the textbooks sometimes sprint past.

prompt: Which Prussian officer helped train the Continental Army at Valley Forge?
explanation: Von Steuben drilled the army into a more disciplined force. Valley Forge got a very intense coach.

prompt: Which young French aristocrat became a major American ally in the Revolutionary War?
explanation: Lafayette became a key ally and symbol of French support. He was basically the Revolution's favorite exchange student.

prompt: "Molly Pitcher" is best understood as what?

choices.text: A nickname/legend linked to women who aided soldiers

explanation: Molly Pitcher is a legendary nickname associated with women such as Mary Ludwig Hays. History loves a composite character.

prompt: The Quartering Acts were mostly about what?

choices.text: Housing British troops in the colonies

explanation: The Quartering Acts dealt with housing and supplying British troops. Colonists were not thrilled about the room-service policy.

prompt: The "Intolerable Acts" were officially known by what less dramatic name?
explanation: Americans called them Intolerable; Britain called them Coercive. Either way, the vibes were bad.

prompt: The "Great Compromise" at the Constitutional Convention created what arrangement?

choices.text: A two-house Congress with different representation rules
Two presidents, one for weekdays and one for weekends

explanation: The compromise helped create the Senate and House structure. The founders solved conflict by inventing more meetings.

prompt: Which state refused to send delegates to the Constitutional Convention?
explanation: Rhode Island skipped the Convention. Even in 1787, someone was declining the calendar invite.

prompt: Which president was the first to live in the White House?

choices.text: The contractor, technically, for one very dusty week

explanation: John Adams moved in before it was fully finished. Nothing says prestige like damp plaster and national responsibility.

prompt: Who burned the White House in 1814?
explanation: British forces burned public buildings in Washington during the War of 1812. The house got a very involuntary renovation.

prompt: Who wrote most of the Declaration of Independence?

choices.text: A committee that wrote only in all caps

explanation: Jefferson drafted most of it, with edits from others. Even founding documents have group-project energy.

prompt: Which group drafted the Declaration before Congress edited and approved it?

choices.text: The Subcommittee on Snacks and Treason

explanation: The Committee of Five included Jefferson, Adams, Franklin, Sherman, and Livingston. Five people, one historic breakup note.

prompt: "Yankee Doodle" began as what kind of song?

choices.text: An advertisement for very patriotic pasta

explanation: The tune was used to mock colonists, who later embraced it. Revolutionary confidence includes stealing the insult and dancing with it.

prompt: The first official U.S. flag resolution in 1777 did NOT specify what?

choices.text: That the colors were red, white, and blue
The exact level of patriotic vibes

explanation: The resolution called for 13 stars and 13 stripes, but not the star pattern. Early flag design had room for jazz.

prompt: On the Great Seal, which way does the eagle face?
explanation: The eagle faces the olive branch, symbolically favoring peace. Still holding arrows, though, because subtlety took the day off.

prompt: Which president operated a large whiskey distillery at Mount Vernon after leaving office?
explanation: Washington's Mount Vernon distillery became one of the largest in America. The father of his country also understood party supplies.

prompt: Benjamin Franklin served as the first person in which national postal role?
explanation: Franklin became the first Postmaster General under the Continental Congress. He was networking before email ruined everything.

prompt: What was "Old Ironsides"?
explanation: Old Ironsides is the nickname of the USS Constitution. The ship earned a nickname with serious superhero posture.

prompt: What was the first U.S. national park?

choices.text: Liberty Lawn, bring your own eagle

explanation: Yellowstone became the first U.S. national park in 1872. America looked at geothermal chaos and said, "protect that."

prompt: How long did the Pony Express operate?

choices.text: Still running, if the horse is motivated

explanation: The Pony Express ran from 1860 to 1861. Its legend has had a much better run than its business model.

prompt: Where is Ellis Island located?

choices.text: Under the Statue of Liberty's basement

explanation: Ellis Island sits in New York Harbor near the Statue of Liberty. Geography put the symbols close together for dramatic effect.

prompt: Which amendment moved presidential inauguration day from March to January?
explanation: The 20th Amendment shortened the lame-duck period and moved inauguration to January 20. The calendar got a constitutional tune-up.

prompt: Who led the expedition that explored much of the Louisiana Purchase?

choices.text: Two guys and one aggressively folded map

explanation: Lewis and Clark led the Corps of Discovery. The road trip had fewer gas stations and more diplomatic complexity.

prompt: Sacagawea is best known for helping which expedition?
explanation: Sacagawea assisted the Lewis and Clark Expedition as interpreter and guide. She was more useful than several maps combined.

prompt: What did the Treaty of Paris of 1783 do?
explanation: The Treaty of Paris formally ended the war and recognized U.S. independence. The breakup became legally official.

prompt: What happened at Lexington and Concord in April 1775?

choices.text: The first major fighting of the Revolutionary War
Tea was dumped with improved aim

explanation: Lexington and Concord marked the outbreak of armed conflict. The argument had officially left the comments section.
